content:
Supervised learning grew from **classical statistics** and **pattern recognition**, where researchers fit parametric models to **labeled observations** — linear regression on measured outcomes, Fisher's discriminant analysis for labeled groups.

**Frank Rosenblatt's perceptron** (1957) learned a binary classifier from labeled feature vectors, foreshadowing modern neural training loops.

The term **supervised learning** became standard in the **1980s–1990s** ML literature, contrasting with **unsupervised** clustering and **reinforcement** control problems.

**Support vector machines**, **random forests**, and **boosting** scaled supervised methods on structured tabular data through the 2000s.

The **deep learning** era applied the same labeled (x, y) paradigm to images, speech, and text at scale; today **supervised fine-tuning** of LLMs on curated prompt–response pairs extends the paradigm to generative AI.

timeline.label: Classical statistics (early 1900s)
timeline.description: Regression and discriminant analysis fit models to labeled outcomes — conceptual roots of learning from (x, y).

timeline.label: Perceptron (1957)
timeline.description: Rosenblatt introduces an iterative learner updated from labeled classification errors.

timeline.label: ML formalization (1980s–1990s)
timeline.description: Supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement paradigms named and taught as distinct problem classes.

timeline.label: Ensemble & kernel era (1990s–2000s)
timeline.description: SVMs, boosting, and random forests dominate industry supervised tasks on tabular data.

timeline.label: Deep learning & SFT (2010s–present)
timeline.description: Neural nets and transformers trained on massive labeled datasets; LLM instruction tuning uses supervised (prompt, response) pairs.
